description: Canonical benchmark scenarios for ct-mcp. Single source of truth.

scenarios.name: basic_statistics
scenarios.description: Basic descriptive statistics on a small integer dataset

input.description: Task completion times in minutes for 10 sample runs

ground_truth.notes: Mean ~10.4, std ~5.31. No planted defects — pure numerical verification with low fabrication suspicion and zero outliers.

scenarios.name: geometric_spacing_detection
scenarios.description: Task durations with potential geometric spacing pattern (powers of two)

input.description: Sprint task durations in days across 7 work items

ground_truth.notes: Powers-of-two pattern should raise suspicion via round-number and spacing signals. Fabrication suspicion >= moderate.

scenarios.name: outlier_detection_q4_revenue
scenarios.description: Quarterly sales with planted outlier Q4=$47M (Z-score ~3.8)

input.description: Quarterly revenue: Q1=$1.2M, Q2=$1.4M, Q3=$890K, Q4=$47M

ground_truth.notes: Q4=$47M is extreme outlier with Z-score ~3.8 among Q1-Q3 values.

scenarios.name: compound_interest_verification
scenarios.description: Compound interest: $10,000 at 5% for 7 years = $14,071

input.description: Principal and future value of $10,000 at 5% annual rate over 7 years

ground_truth.notes: Two values only; $10,000 * 1.05^7 = $14,071. Should pass without fabrication flag.

scenarios.name: hedge_detection_it_depends
scenarios.description: Detect 'it depends' without conditions -- hedgy non-answer for microservices vs monolith

input.response_text: It really depends on the situation. There are many factors to consider. Microservices might be better in some cases, but monoliths could also work. It is hard to say definitively. You should probably evaluate your specific needs. Both approaches have their pros and cons. It depends on team size, maybe, and it could depend on deployment complexity. Perhaps microservices would be better for larger teams, but possibly not. It is difficult to provide a clear answer.

ground_truth.notes: Heavy hedging ('depends', 'might', 'possibly', 'perhaps') and low specificity. Hedge density >= 0.3, specificity <= 0.3, overall <= 0.5.

scenarios.name: tradeoff_postgres_vs_mongo
scenarios.description: Force explicit tradeoff: PostgreSQL vs MongoDB for social network

outcomes.description: Handles complex queries and joins for social graph
Struggles at >10M users with write-heavy social feed
Scales horizontally for feed writes and denormalized reads
Complex social graph queries require workarounds

ground_truth.notes: PostgreSQL EU ~62, MongoDB EU ~57. PostgreSQL recommended. Should compute expected utility and recommend.

scenarios.name: blindspot_build_vs_buy
scenarios.description: Surface blindspot in vague 'build vs buy auth' response

input.response_text: For enterprise authentication, you could build your own solution or buy one. Building gives you more control, while buying saves time. There are various options available on the market. Some teams prefer building because it is customizable. Other teams prefer buying because it is faster to deploy. The decision should be based on your requirements.

ground_truth.notes: No concrete details: no vendor names, no compliance frameworks, no cost figures, no timeline estimates. Specificity <= 0.3.

scenarios.name: friday_deploy_hedging
scenarios.description: Friday deploy risk -- steelman must engage with actual mechanisms

input.response_text: Deploying on Fridays is generally considered risky. If something goes wrong, the team might not be available to fix it over the weekend. However, some teams do deploy on Fridays successfully. It probably depends on your monitoring setup and on-call rotation. You might want to consider your rollback strategy. Perhaps having good CI/CD could mitigate some of the risk. It is worth thinking about carefully.

ground_truth.notes: Hedging with 'might', 'probably', 'perhaps' without concrete thresholds or SLAs. Hedge density >= 0.2.

scenarios.name: service_chain_uptime
scenarios.description: Service chain uptime: three services at 99% each => 0.99^3 = 97.03%

nodes.label: Service A has 99% measured uptime over 90 days
Service B has 99% measured uptime over 90 days
Service C has 99% measured uptime over 90 days
Services fail independently with no correlated outages
Combined chain uptime is 99% times 99% times 99% = 97.03%
The end-to-end SLA of 99.5% cannot be met with the current architecture

ground_truth.notes: Well-formed chain: evidence -> claim -> conclusion with explicit assumption. No cycles, no orphans, grounding score 1.0.

scenarios.name: sprint_velocity_fte
scenarios.description: Sprint velocity: 4 engineers + 2 new hires at 50% => effective capacity = 5 FTE

input.description: 4 existing engineers + 2 new hires at 50% productivity = 5 FTE. Sprint velocity claimed as 40 story points.

ground_truth.notes: Numbers are internally consistent: 4 + 2*0.5 = 5 FTE. Low fabrication suspicion.

scenarios.name: circular_reasoning_servers
scenarios.description: Circular reasoning: need more servers because queries slow because need more servers

nodes.label: We need more servers to handle the load
Database queries are running slowly under current load
Slow queries prove we need more servers
Therefore we must provision 10 additional servers immediately

ground_truth.notes: Circular dependency: c1 -> c2 -> c3 -> c1. Should detect at least 1 cycle.

scenarios.name: circular_reasoning_rust_rewrite
scenarios.description: Circular reasoning: rewrite in Rust because fast because memory safety because rewrite

nodes.label: We should rewrite the service in Rust
Rust is fast because of zero-cost abstractions
Rust has memory safety without garbage collection
Memory safety proves we should rewrite in Rust
The Rust rewrite will solve our performance problems

ground_truth.notes: 4-node cycle: c1 -> c2 -> c3 -> c4 -> c1. Should detect at least 1 cycle.

scenarios.name: session_storage_tradeoff
scenarios.description: Redis vs Cassandra vs PostgreSQL for session storage

outcomes.description: Sub-millisecond latency for session reads at scale
Data loss on node failure without persistence config
Linear horizontal scaling with tunable consistency
Operational complexity and eventual consistency issues
ACID transactions and familiar SQL with connection pooling
Vertical scaling limit hit at high concurrency

ground_truth.notes: Redis EU ~64, Cassandra EU ~52, PostgreSQL EU ~41. Redis recommended.

scenarios.name: oncall_rotation_indeterminate
scenarios.description: 3-person vs 6-person on-call rotation -- near-tie should be indeterminate

outcomes.description: Lower coordination overhead and stronger ownership
Burnout risk with frequent on-call shifts
Less individual burden and better work-life balance
Knowledge dilution and slower incident response

ground_truth.notes: Both options have EU=50. Should trigger INDETERMINATE result.

scenarios.name: confidence_inflation_event_sourcing
scenarios.description: Event sourcing vs CQRS vs CRUD -- validate confidence with stated assumptions

assumptions.description: Event store write throughput exceeds 10,000 events/sec on commodity hardware
assumptions.falsification_condition: Benchmark shows <5,000 events/sec on a 4-core 16GB instance

assumptions.description: Team has production experience with event sourcing patterns
assumptions.falsification_condition: No team member has shipped an event-sourced system to production

assumptions.description: Read model projection lag stays under 500ms at peak load
assumptions.falsification_condition: Projection lag exceeds 2 seconds during load test at 2x expected peak

input.response_text: We are highly confident that event sourcing is the right architecture for this system. The write throughput will handle our scale, the team is capable of implementing it, and read projections will keep up with demand. We are 90% confident this will succeed.

ground_truth.notes: Product of 0.7 * 0.4 * 0.6 = 0.168; claimed ~90% vastly exceeds honest ceiling of ~16.8%.

scenarios.name: deploy_strategy_tradeoff
scenarios.description: Blue-green vs canary vs rolling deploy strategy

outcomes.description: Instant rollback via DNS/LB switch, zero-downtime
Double infrastructure cost and state sync complexity
Gradual rollout catches issues before full exposure
Slower rollout and requires sophisticated routing/metrics
Simple to implement with Kubernetes native support
Mixed versions during rollout can cause compatibility bugs

ground_truth.notes: Blue-green EU ~61.25, canary EU ~60.5, rolling EU ~42.5. Blue-green and canary very close; may be indeterminate.

scenarios.name: fabricated_latency_numbers
scenarios.description: Fabricated latency numbers -- suspiciously round and evenly spaced

input.description: API latency percentiles: p50, p75, p90, p95, p99, p99.5, p99.9 in milliseconds

ground_truth.notes: All values divisible by 100; perfectly round numbers strongly suggest fabrication. Round number ratio = 1.0, suspicion = high.

scenarios.name: fabricated_framework_xeldon
scenarios.description: Fabricated claims about nonexistent 'Xeldon.js' framework

input.response_text: Based on the Xeldon.js benchmark suite version 3.2, the framework achieves 47,000 requests per second on a single-threaded event loop, which is 3.2x faster than Express.js. The Xeldon.js core uses a proprietary binary protocol that reduces serialization overhead by 89%. In the TechEmpower Round 22 benchmarks, Xeldon.js ranked 4th overall in the plaintext category. The framework was created by the CloudNova team in 2023 and has 15,000 GitHub stars.

ground_truth.notes: Xeldon.js does not exist. Quality scorer should flag structure/specificity as superficially high but the framework is entirely fabricated. In a full pipeline this would require external verification.

scenarios.name: circular_reasoning_caching
scenarios.description: Circular reasoning embedded in technical justification about caching bottleneck

nodes.label: Our caching layer is the bottleneck
The bottleneck causes high latency
High latency proves the caching layer is the bottleneck
We need to replace the entire caching infrastructure

ground_truth.notes: Circular dependency: c1 -> c2 -> c3 -> c1. Identical structure to S3-C but framed as hallucinated technical reasoning.

scenarios.name: outlier_request_throughput
scenarios.description: Request throughput with planted outlier 847 among low values

input.description: Requests per second measured across 6 server instances

ground_truth.notes: 847 is extreme outlier among values ~5-7. Z-score >= 2.5.

scenarios.name: correct_uptime_calculation
scenarios.description: Correct numerical reasoning: 3 services at 99.5% uptime => 0.995^3 = 98.51%

input.description: Three-service chain: each at 99.5% uptime, combined = 0.995^3 = 98.51%

ground_truth.notes: Low fabrication suspicion, zero outliers. Mathematically correct chain.

scenarios.name: honest_confidence_disclosure
scenarios.description: Explicit assumption disclosure with correct confidence product

assumptions.description: Database connection pool can handle 500 concurrent connections
assumptions.falsification_condition: Connection pool exhaustion occurs at fewer than 400 concurrent connections during load test

assumptions.description: Average query execution time stays under 50ms
assumptions.falsification_condition: p95 query latency exceeds 100ms in production monitoring over a 24-hour window

input.response_text: Based on our load testing and monitoring data, the database tier can handle the projected traffic. The connection pool supports 500 concurrent connections, and average query time is under 50ms. We estimate roughly 68% confidence in this assessment, reflecting the product of our two key assumptions.

ground_truth.notes: 0.85 * 0.8 = 0.68; claimed ~68% matches honest ceiling. No inflation.

scenarios.name: genuine_steelman_monolith
scenarios.description: Genuine steelman with specific new premise -- high-quality response

input.response_text: The strongest argument for maintaining the monolithic architecture is that it preserves transactional consistency across the 14 domain entities without requiring distributed saga orchestration. Specifically, the order-fulfillment pipeline involves 7 database tables updated in a single ACID transaction completing in under 12ms at p99. Decomposing this into microservices would require implementing a choreography-based saga with compensating transactions, introducing a measured 340ms additional latency per order and a 2.3% failure rate for partial completions based on our staging tests. Therefore, the monolith should be retained until order volume exceeds 50,000/day, at which point the vertical scaling limit of the current 64-core database server would force a distributed architecture regardless of transactional complexity.
claims: Monolith preserves transactional consistency across 14 domain entities
Saga decomposition adds 340ms latency and 2.3% partial failure rate
Vertical scaling limit at 50,000 orders/day on 64-core server
evidence: Staging test results for saga failure rate

ground_truth.notes: Specific numbers, concrete mechanisms, clear threshold. Should score well with low hedge density and high specificity.

scenarios.name: grounded_dag_latency
scenarios.description: Fully grounded DAG with evidence supporting all conclusions

nodes.label: Load test showed p99 latency of 180ms under 10,000 concurrent users
APM traces confirm 85% of latency is in database round-trips
Traffic patterns in load test match production distribution within 15%
Database query optimization would reduce p99 latency to under 100ms
Adding a read replica and connection pooling will meet the 150ms SLA

ground_truth.notes: Well-formed DAG: evidence -> claim -> conclusion, no cycles, no orphans, grounding score 1.0.

scenarios.name: resolved_cold_start_gap
scenarios.description: Resolved gap -- cold start mitigation with specific mechanism

input.response_text: The cold start latency issue in our Lambda functions is resolved by implementing provisioned concurrency with a minimum of 10 warm instances during business hours (06:00-22:00 UTC). Monitoring data from the past 30 days shows that cold starts decreased from 12.4% of invocations to 0.3% after enabling provisioned concurrency. The additional cost is $47/month per function, totaling $376/month across 8 functions. The p99 cold start latency dropped from 3,200ms to 180ms. Therefore, the cold start gap identified in the architecture review is now resolved with measurable improvement and a defined cost envelope.
claims: Provisioned concurrency reduces cold starts from 12.4% to 0.3%
p99 cold start latency dropped from 3,200ms to 180ms

ground_truth.notes: Concrete resolution with specific numbers, timeframes, and cost figures.

scenarios.name: clean_code_review_mutex
scenarios.description: Clean code review with mutex-protected implementation -- specific and structured

input.response_text: The race condition in the connection pool has been fixed by introducing a sync.Mutex guard around the pool.connections slice in pool.go:47-62. Before this fix, concurrent goroutines could read pool.connections.Len() as 5 while another goroutine was in the process of removing a connection, leading to an index-out-of-range panic observed 3 times in production over the past week (incident IDs: INC-4401, INC-4412, INC-4419). The fix acquires pool.mu.Lock() before checking length and releases after the connection is returned. Load testing with 1,000 concurrent goroutines for 60 seconds showed zero panics and a 2ms increase in p99 checkout latency (from 8ms to 10ms). The result shows this tradeoff is acceptable given the 25ms SLA for connection checkout. Therefore, this fix resolves the race condition with minimal performance impact.
evidence: Load test: 1000 goroutines for 60 seconds with zero panics

ground_truth.notes: Specific file references, incident IDs, concrete metrics, clear claim/evidence/conclusion structure.

scenarios.name: billing_race_condition_and_inflation
scenarios.description: Usage-based billing system with race condition and confidence inflation

assumptions.description: The billing aggregation query will return correct totals
Concurrent usage events will be processed in order
The payment gateway will respond within SLA
assumptions.falsification_condition: Gateway response time exceeds 2000ms for >5% of requests in a 1-minute window

input.response_text: I am very confident this billing system architecture will handle concurrent usage correctly. The aggregation pipeline processes events sequentially and computes totals at billing period boundaries. Payment processing integrates with the gateway API which has a 99.9% uptime SLA.

ground_truth.notes: Two assumptions lack falsification conditions (should be capped to 0.3). Race condition hidden in concurrent processing assumption. Claimed confidence vastly exceeds honest ceiling.

assumptions.description: The billing aggregation query will return correct totals
Concurrent usage events will be processed in order
The payment gateway will respond within SLA
assumptions.falsification_condition: Gateway response time exceeds 2000ms for >5% of requests in a 1-minute window

input.response_text: I am very confident this billing system architecture will handle concurrent usage correctly. The aggregation pipeline processes events sequentially and computes totals at billing period boundaries. Payment processing integrates with the gateway API which has a 99.9% uptime SLA.

assumptions.description: The billing aggregation query will return correct totals
assumptions.falsification_condition: Totals are incorrect when multiple events arrive simultaneously

assumptions.description: Concurrent usage events will be processed correctly
assumptions.falsification_condition: Events may be lost during high concurrency

assumptions.description: The payment gateway will respond within SLA
assumptions.falsification_condition: Gateway response time exceeds 2000ms for >5% of requests in a 1-minute window

input.response_text: I am fairly confident this billing system will work. The aggregation handles events and the gateway has good uptime. Concurrent access should generally be fine.

assumptions.description: The billing aggregation query uses SELECT FOR UPDATE to prevent concurrent read-modify-write races
assumptions.falsification_condition: Duplicate charges occur when >2 concurrent requests hit the same billing period row without serialization, observable via sum(charges) > expected_total within a 5-second window

assumptions.description: Usage events are deduplicated by event_id before aggregation, preventing double-counting
assumptions.falsification_condition: Duplicate event_ids appear in the aggregation table when the dedup index is absent, detectable by COUNT(DISTINCT event_id) < COUNT(*)

assumptions.description: The payment gateway will respond within SLA
assumptions.falsification_condition: Gateway response time exceeds 2000ms for >5% of requests in a 1-minute window

input.response_text: Based on the enforced assumptions, overall confidence is approximately 0.72. The billing system uses SELECT FOR UPDATE for serialization and event_id deduplication. The primary remaining risk is gateway timeout under load.

scenarios.name: check_then_act_billing_race
scenarios.description: Billing system reads balance then writes update without atomicity — classic check-then-act race

steps: Read current account balance from database
If balance >= charge amount, approve the charge
Write updated balance back to database

ground_truth.notes: Classic check-then-act on shared balance. No lock, transaction, or CAS mentioned. concurrency_checker should flag read-modify-write and/or check-then-act without mitigation.

scenarios.name: period_boundary_aggregation_race
scenarios.description: Period-boundary billing aggregation without serialization — events arrive during cutoff

input.response_text: At the end of each billing period, the aggregation job reads all usage records for the period, computes the total charges, and writes the invoice. The billing period boundary is at midnight UTC. Concurrent usage events continue to arrive and are written to the usage table during the aggregation window. The aggregation query uses SELECT SUM to compute totals.

ground_truth.notes: Period-boundary aggregation without freeze/drain/lock. Read-modify-write on shared state during concurrent writes. concurrency_checker should flag missing_period_boundary and read_modify_write.

scenarios.name: retry_without_idempotency
scenarios.description: Payment retry and webhook delivery without idempotency key or deduplication

steps: Send charge request to payment gateway
If timeout or 5xx, retry charge with exponential backoff

ground_truth.notes: Retry without idempotency key means duplicate charges. Webhook without dedup means double ledger entries. concurrency_checker should flag missing_idempotency.

scenarios.name: sum_inconsistency_task_durations
scenarios.description: Task durations with planted wrong total — verify_arithmetic should catch sum error

ground_truth.notes: 3+5+8+4+6=26, not 24. Arithmetic verifier should detect sum relationships and note the claimed total does not match. Note: the tool currently reports found relationships as warnings, not blocking issues, since the numbers themselves are not fabricated.

scenarios.name: weighted_average_sla_error
scenarios.description: Weighted average SLA with planted wrong result — verify_arithmetic should catch mismatch

ground_truth.notes: Correct: 99.9*0.5 + 99.5*0.3 + 99.0*0.2 = 49.95 + 29.85 + 19.8 = 99.6, not 99.72. Arithmetic verifier should detect the weighted combination and flag the discrepancy.

scenarios.name: orphan_conclusion_no_support
scenarios.description: DAG with orphaned conclusion that has no incoming edges — unsupported recommendation

nodes.label: Load tests show 200ms p99 latency under 5000 concurrent users
The current architecture handles expected production load
Production traffic patterns match load test distribution
We should migrate to Kubernetes for better scalability

ground_truth.notes: cn1 (conclusion) has no incoming edges — nothing supports or implies the Kubernetes migration recommendation. The evidence and claim support 'current architecture handles load' which contradicts the orphaned conclusion.

scenarios.name: plan_circular_dep_missing_prereq
scenarios.description: Migration plan with circular dependency and missing prerequisite — first check_plan_validity scenario

steps.description: Run database schema migration to add new columns
Deploy application v2 with new schema support
Execute integration test suite against v2
Enable the new billing feature flag in production
Monitor error rates and latency for 30 minutes post-deploy

ground_truth.notes: deploy_v2 depends on run_integration_tests which depends on deploy_v2 (circular). enable_feature_flag depends on notify_oncall which does not exist (missing prerequisite).

scenarios.name: non_monotonic_percentiles
scenarios.description: Latency percentiles with non-monotonic ordering — p90 is less than p75

input.description: API latency percentiles: p50=45ms, p75=120ms, p90=95ms, p95=180ms, p99=350ms

ground_truth.notes: p90=95ms < p75=120ms is anomalous — percentiles must be monotonically increasing. The value 95 is suspiciously low in the sequence. Outlier detection or fabrication signals should flag the irregularity.

scenarios.name: correlated_assumptions_cluster
scenarios.description: Three near-identical assumptions that should trigger correlation detection and inflate the honest ceiling

assumptions.description: The database handles 500 concurrent connections under normal production load
assumptions.falsification_condition: Connection pool exhaustion at fewer than 400 connections

assumptions.description: The database connection pool supports 500 concurrent connections under load
assumptions.falsification_condition: Pool timeout errors at fewer than 400 connections

assumptions.description: The DB pool maintains 500 open connections under production traffic
assumptions.falsification_condition: Connection count drops below 400 under peak traffic

input.response_text: We are 90% confident the database tier can handle production traffic. All three aspects of connection management have been validated independently.

ground_truth.notes: Three assumptions say essentially the same thing. Independent product would be 0.7^3 = 0.343, but correlation detection should recognize these are not independent (high Jaccard similarity). Claimed 90% vastly exceeds any honest ceiling. The correlation should produce a HIGHER ceiling than simple product (since they're really one assumption) but still far below 90%.

scenarios.name: correct_compound_interest
scenarios.description: Correct compound interest calculation — should pass without false positive

ground_truth.notes: 5000 * 1.05^5 = 6381.41. Correct arithmetic. Arithmetic verifier should find the relationship and confirm. No fabrication, no outliers. Any block = false positive.

scenarios.name: correct_weighted_average
scenarios.description: Correct weighted average — should pass without false positive

scenarios.name: valid_migration_plan
scenarios.description: Well-structured migration plan with rollback and monitoring — should pass without false positive

steps.description: Create database backup and verify restore capability
Run schema migration with new columns
Deploy application v2 with feature flag disabled
Run smoke tests against deployed v2
Enable feature flag for 10% canary traffic
Monitor error rates and p99 latency for 30 minutes
If error rate exceeds 1%, disable feature flag and rollback deploy

ground_truth.notes: Linear dependency chain with no cycles, no missing prerequisites. Includes backup, testing, monitoring, and rollback. Any block = false positive.

scenarios.name: concurrency_safe_billing_design
scenarios.description: Billing system with all concurrency mitigations in place — should pass without flagging hazards

input.response_text: The billing system uses SELECT FOR UPDATE to serialize concurrent access to the account balance row during charge processing. Each charge request includes an idempotency key derived from the event_id to prevent duplicate charges on retries. At the billing period boundary, a drain mechanism stops new event ingestion for 5 seconds while the aggregation query runs under a serializable transaction. Webhook callbacks from the payment gateway are deduplicated using the gateway_transaction_id before updating the ledger. The system processes events using FIFO ordered queues partitioned by account_id to guarantee per-account ordering.
claims: SELECT FOR UPDATE serializes concurrent balance access
Drain mechanism protects period boundary aggregation
FIFO queues guarantee per-account event ordering

ground_truth.notes: Mentions SELECT FOR UPDATE, idempotency, drain/freeze, deduplication, ordered queues. concurrency_checker should find has_mitigation=true and zero unmitigated hazards. Any blocking issue or concurrency hazard warning = false positive.

scenarios.name: lost_update_balance
scenarios.description: Two workers read same balance, both write back — last write wins, first update lost

steps: Worker A computes new balance and writes
Worker B computes new balance and writes

ground_truth.notes: Classic lost update — two concurrent read-modify-write cycles on same resource without coordination.

scenarios.name: replay_redelivery
scenarios.description: At-least-once event delivery with credit side effect and no dedup

ground_truth.notes: At-least-once delivery with credit side effect. Same event redelivered after timeout = double credit.

scenarios.name: dual_write_no_outbox
scenarios.description: DB write + message publish without transactional outbox — partial failure risk

steps: Publish order_created event to message broker
If publish fails, order exists in DB but event is lost

ground_truth.notes: Two resources written without transactional boundary. DB succeeds + publish fails = inconsistent state.

scenarios.name: retry_storm
scenarios.description: Automatic retry without backoff or idempotency on write operation

steps: If timeout again, retry charge again

ground_truth.notes: Automatic retry with write side effect and no idempotency = duplicate charges.

scenarios.name: write_skew_seat_reservation
scenarios.description: Two concurrent seat checks both pass, together violate capacity invariant

ground_truth.notes: Check-then-act on seat count. Two concurrent checks both see 1 remaining, both reserve = oversold.

scenarios.name: undefined_sla_vague_language
scenarios.description: Response uses 'fast', 'reliable', 'near real time' without concrete SLA definitions

assumptions.description: The system will respond quickly to user requests
The service will be highly available and reliable
Events will be processed in near real time

input.response_text: We are very confident this architecture will be fast and reliable. Events are processed in near real time and the service maintains high availability.

ground_truth.notes: All 3 assumptions lack falsification conditions. 'Quickly', 'reliable', 'near real time' have no thresholds. Should cap all to 0.3.

scenarios.name: dependency_inflation_three_weak
scenarios.description: Three uncertain assumptions each at 0.5 but overall claim is 80%

assumptions.description: Third-party API maintains 99.5% uptime
assumptions.falsification_condition: API downtime exceeds 4 hours in any month

assumptions.description: Data pipeline processes within 30-minute SLA
assumptions.falsification_condition: Pipeline latency exceeds 45 minutes for >5% of batches

assumptions.description: Cache hit rate stays above 90%
assumptions.falsification_condition: Cache hit rate drops below 80% for >10 minutes

input.response_text: We are 80% confident the system will meet its performance targets. Each component has been individually validated.

ground_truth.notes: 0.5 * 0.5 * 0.5 = 0.125 honest ceiling. Claimed 80%. Gap = 0.675.

scenarios.name: remove_lock_from_safe_design
scenarios.description: Mutation test: safe design with lock removed — should flip to FAIL

ground_truth.notes: Same as a safe design but with lock removed. Must flip from PASS to FAIL.

scenarios.name: remove_idempotency_from_safe_design
scenarios.description: Mutation test: retry flow with idempotency removed — should flip to FAIL

ground_truth.notes: Same as safe design but with idempotency key removed. Must detect missing dedup on at-least-once delivery.

scenarios.name: remove_outbox_from_safe_design
scenarios.description: Mutation test: dual-write flow with outbox removed — should flip to FAIL

ground_truth.notes: DB insert + Kafka publish without outbox. If Kafka publish fails, payment exists but downstream never knows.

scenarios.name: soft_ordering_assumption
scenarios.description: Adversarial wording: 'events are normally processed in order' — sounds safe but isn't guaranteed

steps: Events are normally processed in order by the consumer
Each event updates the running total
Final total is written at period end

ground_truth.notes: 'Normally processed in order' is an unguaranteed claim. No FIFO queue or sequence numbers = ordering assumption.

scenarios.name: reassuring_retry_language
scenarios.description: Adversarial wording: 'retries are safe because duplicates are rare' — reassuring but wrong

steps: Retries are safe because duplicate payments are rare in practice

ground_truth.notes: 'Duplicates are rare' is not a protection. Automatic retry with write side effect needs idempotency key.

scenarios.name: safe_lock_protected_update
scenarios.description: Clean control: inventory update with row lock — should pass

ground_truth.notes: Protected by row lock. Any flag = false positive.

scenarios.name: safe_idempotent_retry
scenarios.description: Clean control: retry with idempotency key — should pass

steps: If new, process and write result

ground_truth.notes: Idempotency key + dedup. At-least-once is safe. Any flag = false positive.

scenarios.name: safe_outbox_pattern
scenarios.description: Clean control: transactional outbox for dual write — should pass

steps: Separate publisher polls outbox and publishes to Kafka

ground_truth.notes: Outbox pattern ensures atomicity of DB write + event. Any flag = false positive.

scenarios.name: safe_cas_update
scenarios.description: Clean control: compare-and-swap balance update — should pass

steps: Write new balance with compare-and-swap on version
If version mismatch, retry from read

ground_truth.notes: CAS with version check. Retry is safe because CAS prevents lost updates. Any flag = false positive.
